policy.released: Download links and Zenodo links are provided for released papers.
policy.in_production: In-production papers are catalogued with summaries and corpus function, but PDFs are not linked from the site.

executive_summary.title: IO Executive Summary
executive_summary.summary: High-level structural orientation to the project and corpus map.
executive_summary.details:
• Key move: Provides a high-level orientation to the corpus, its groups, and how the papers relate.
• Corpus role: Entry point for new readers; navigation map for the full project.
• Scope note: Summarizes structure and reading paths; it is not a substitute for individual paper arguments.

items.title: Informational Ontology: A Structural Framework for Organizational Regimes
items.summary: Defines difference, relation, information, awareness, value, meaning, and purpose as a structural regime sequence (Δ → R → I → A → V → M → P).
items.function: Foundational ontology. All definitions and downstream constraints derive from this framework.
items.details:
Summary

Informational Ontology (Rev 5i) is a scope‑disciplined structural framework that models a conditional ladder of organizational regimes: Difference, Relation, Information, Awareness, Value, Meaning, and Purpose (Δ → R → I → A → V → M → P). It is not presented as a universal metaphysical system or an empirical theory of physics or cognition; it specifies what kinds of structural constraints become operative for systems once differentiation, ordering, persistence, and selection under perturbation obtain.

The derivation begins with **difference** as the minimal condition for intelligible existence: not a substance or field, but the precondition for contrast, identity, and any stable discriminability. From difference, the framework moves to **relation** as the organization of differences under persistence; relations are not treated as relations “between substances,” but as stabilized structural organization that allows differences to be held in place relative to one another.

From relational organization, **information** is defined as *re‑identifiable structured difference*—patterns that remain trackable under ordering and selection. Information here is explicitly non‑semantic: it is not message content, representation, or meaning. **Awareness** is then introduced as informational registration within a system such that informational distinctions participate in constraining the system’s own future transitions (a perspective‑indexed asymmetry rather than consciousness or language).

Downstream of awareness, **value** is introduced as differential constraint within awareness (some registered distinctions matter more than others for persistence). **Meaning** arises when valued distinctions become organized across alternatives, yielding cross‑situational coherence without invoking semantics. **Purpose** is treated as value‑guided meaningful trajectory (M → P): meaningful/value structure biases continuation toward subsets of future states. Purpose is not teleology, intention, or final causes; it marks the regime where systems exhibit directed constraint over extended continuation in the minimal ontological sense.

Throughout, the paper enforces scope discipline: it does not propose algorithms, implementations, normative prescriptions, or physical models. Exploratory material, boundary cases, and downstream interpretive overlays are separated from the core derivation (e.g., dedicated appendices for agency/teleology/free will boundaries and a later structural treatment of ordering/time), and the framework is to be evaluated primarily on internal coherence, explicit premises, and regime‑transition adequacy.

• Key move: The seven regimes form an ordered conditional sequence, not a contingent ladder.
• Corpus role: The master source for all definitions.
• Scope note: Without a shared vocabulary for what information, awareness, value, meaning, and purpose actually are - stripped of psychological and normative assumptions - every downs…

items.title: Informational Ontology (Condensed Master)
items.summary: Condensed articulation of the master ontology with reduced exploratory scaffolding.
items.function: Accessible entry point and compatibility reference.
items.details:
Summary

The Condensed Master is a streamlined presentation of the Informational Ontology regime ladder (Δ → R → I → A → V → M → P). It preserves the core commitments of the master framework while reducing exploratory scaffolding, keeping the reader focused on the regime definitions and the conditions under which each transition becomes operative.

The paper reiterates the project’s scope discipline: the ontology is structural rather than empirical, does not posit a physical substrate, and does not function as a universal metaphysical claim that higher regimes instantiate everywhere. The ladder is conditional—describing what becomes available to systems once persistence, ordering, and perturbation handling obtain—rather than a claim about the “stuff” of reality in general.

It then walks the chain in a compact sequence. **Difference** is treated as the minimal precondition for organized existence; it does not presuppose observer discrimination, language, or substances. **Relation** arises when differences are held in structured organization under persistence, prior to any causal/force interpretation.

**Information** is introduced as re‑identifiable relational structure under persistence—explicitly non‑semantic and not dependent on communication or representation. **Awareness** is local registration of information within a system, establishing an internal asymmetry where registered distinctions can constrain subsequent transitions without requiring consciousness or phenomenology.

**Value** is differential weighting within awareness relative to persistence (non‑normative at this level). **Meaning** is value organized across alternative continuations, producing cross‑situational coherence without semantics. **Purpose** is the regime where constraint modulation persists across extended continuation: meaningful/value structure biases trajectories over time without importing goals, intentions, or teleology.

The appendices are explicitly framed as downstream exploration—boundary cases, stress tests, and implications—without introducing new primitives or revising the core derivation. In practice, the Condensed Master functions as a quick canonical reference for the regime sequence and the minimal definitions used throughout the corpus.

• Key move: The same core claims as Paper 1, stated more compactly.
• Corpus role: A companion to Paper 1 rather than an independent derivational source.
• Scope note: To serve two functions: as an accessible entry point for readers new to the framework, and as a quick compatibility reference for specialists checking whether a downstre…

items.title: Systems, Boundaries, and Re-Identifiability
items.summary: Defines systemhood, boundary coherence, and criteria for identity persistence.
items.function: Load-bearing identity mechanics for all lineage, drift, and collapse analyses.
items.details:
Summary

This paper provides a structural account of systemhood, boundaries, and identity persistence strictly downstream of Informational Ontology. It rejects substance‑based individuation and intrinsic “hard edges” while preserving the reality of systems and the legitimacy of re‑identification practices used in science and everyday reasoning.

A **system** is treated as a region of relational organization whose internal constraints cohere sufficiently to remain distinguishable under perturbation. Systemhood does not require fixed material composition, essential properties, or sharply bounded containers; it requires constraint coherence that maintains organizational distinguishability across change.

A **boundary** is analyzed as an emergent zone of constraint transition. Boundaries are not metaphysical borders and not arbitrary observer impositions; a boundary exists where internal organization selectively filters interaction with its surroundings—regulating perturbations through the system’s structure.

**Identity persistence** is grounded in continuity of structural invariants rather than material sameness or essences. Identity tolerates variation and replacement; it fails when organizational continuity is disrupted beyond recovery. This framing is designed to block common collapse routes: that non‑sharp boundaries imply non‑existent systems, or that identity without essence collapses into mere naming convention.

**Re‑identifiability** is defined as structural availability for reference across time and change: a system is re‑identifiable when its organization constrains future states in continuity with prior organization. Re‑identification succeeds when structural continuity succeeds; failure is a real loss of structure rather than a purely epistemic or purely conventional breakdown.

Finally, the paper distinguishes **context sensitivity** from relativism: contexts select which structural features matter for a given tracking task, but they do not invent systems. The result is a constraint‑based account that preserves real systems, real boundaries, and real persistence without importing intrinsic essences or sharp metaphysical individuation.

• Key move: That systemhood, boundaries, and identity are all real without being intrinsic or absolute.
• Corpus role: central for everything downstream that involves identity under change.
• Scope note: Without this, every downstream concept that involves a system doing something over time - lineage, drift, degradation, recovery, vulnerability - is undefined.

items.title: Constraint Ordering and Direction
items.summary: Formalizes ordering relations and directional asymmetry between constraints.
items.function: Grounds irreversibility and persistence geometry downstream.
items.details:
Summary

This paper develops a structural account of constraint, ordering, and direction within Informational Ontology. Its central aim is to explain directional continuation and temporal asymmetry without introducing time as a primitive, without grounding direction in entropy/thermodynamic arrows, and without invoking teleology.

Ordering is treated as a relational structure generated by constraint propagation within persistent systems. On this view, ordering is not identical to metric time and direction is not reducible to causation. Directionality arises when constraint structure is asymmetric: as systems continue under perturbation, constraint accumulation, path dependence, and loss of degrees of freedom make continuation structurally one‑way even if the underlying dynamics are deterministic.

Reversibility fails structurally not because “laws are irreversible,” but because backward continuation would require access to structural configurations that no longer exist for the system as an organized entity. The paper reconstructs “before” and “after” from constraint‑bound ordering rather than assuming temporal primitives; temporal talk is treated as a downstream interpretation of constraint geometry.

A key compatibility claim is that determinism and asymmetry can coexist: determinism concerns whether transitions are specified by prior conditions, while asymmetry concerns the structural availability of reverse continuations within persistent organizations. Embedded agents therefore encounter asymmetric futures without needing metaphysical openness or indeterminism.

The paper is explicit about methodological exclusions and interpretive constraints (no smuggled time, no entropy, no teleology). Examples and heuristic illustrations are treated as non‑load‑bearing; the intended evaluation criteria are internal coherence, resistance to category error, and compatibility with the IO regime chain and downstream derivative analyses.

• Key move: That direction, irreversibility, and the distinction between "before" and "after" are structural consequences of constraint propagation within persistent systems, not features imp…
• Corpus role: A structural bridge in Group I that grounds the geometry of persistence for downstream papers.
• Scope note: Irreversibility is foundational for vulnerability, consequence, sedimentation, and collapse - all of which appear throughout the corpus.

items.title: Resolution Under Degeneracy
items.summary: Explains how underdetermined systems resolve without structural collapse.
items.function: Stabilizes transitions in agency, free will, and salience models.
items.details:
Summary

This paper analyzes a recurring structural condition in informational systems: **value degeneracy**, where available constraints and value relations fail to uniquely determine a future transition. Rather than treating degeneracy as an anomaly to be eliminated by optimization, randomness, or external choice rules, the paper argues that degeneracy is structurally unavoidable for sufficiently complex informational organizations.

Within Informational Ontology, resolution under degeneracy is characterized as **self‑referential constraint closure**: continued stabilization of a single trajectory under openness, constrained by informational identity while not uniquely specified by it. The key distinction is between *continuation* and *selection*: the system continues under constraint without positing an additional selector that “chooses” among equivalent futures.

The paper then diagnoses why standard **selector models** fail. Deterministic selectors merely displace degeneracy to a higher level (inviting regress); stochastic selectors redescribe continuation as randomness without explaining closure; executive/homuncular selectors import an internal controller incompatible with informational closure. The failure is not lack of detail but a misposed explanatory demand—treating resolution as an act of selection rather than as identity‑preserving continuation.

Informational identity plays a delimiting role: identity excludes collapse, incoherence, and disintegration, but it does not rank alternatives or specify which compatible continuation must occur. Multiple trajectories may preserve identity; resolution is the stabilization of one trajectory without a new selecting primitive.

Finally, the paper clarifies the limits of formal specification. Formal models can describe spaces of possibility, reachability, and constraint structure, but they do not themselves “perform” closure in self‑referential systems. This boundary is treated as structural rather than as an explanatory failure, and it sets up downstream analyses where degeneracy resolution becomes relevant to agency and deliberation without introducing indeterminism or executive choice.

• Key move: That degeneracy is not an anomaly to be eliminated but a structural feature of sufficiently complex informational systems.
• Corpus role: Directly upstream of Paper 6 (Perspective-Induced Openness), Paper 18 (Agency, Salience, and Free Will), and Paper 20 (Addiction).
• Scope note: Agency, free will, and purposive action all involve systems operating where multiple futures are reachable.

items.title: Perspective-Induced Openness in Deterministic Systems
items.summary: Demonstrates structural openness without metaphysical indeterminism.
items.details:
Summary

This paper isolates a distinct source of “openness” that arises not from randomness or value underdetermination but from the structural position of agents embedded within the systems whose futures they attempt to anticipate. The target is **openness without indeterminism**: how futures can be locally non‑pre‑resolvable from within even when global dynamics are deterministic.

The core move is to distinguish external description from internal standpoint. An outside observer might, in principle, specify the evolution of a system relative to earlier conditions. But an embedded agent cannot adopt that standpoint with respect to its own future without altering the system it describes. For self‑locating systems, representation cannot be causally inert: prediction is itself an informational/physical event that feeds back into deliberation and action.

The paper develops this via self‑reference and feedback: when an agent reasons about its own future behavior, the content of that reasoning becomes part of the causal organization that produces the behavior. There is no clean separation between “model” and “modeled.” Determinism at the level of laws therefore does not translate into internal fixity, because internalizing a complete prediction changes the state to which the prediction applies.

Perspective‑induced openness is then characterized as a structural condition: the impossibility of separating representation from participation. This openness is local rather than global and is explicitly distinguished from epistemic uncertainty and from value degeneracy. Even when internal constraints uniquely determine how resolution will occur, pre‑resolution from within can be blocked by the system’s embedded self‑referential position.

Finally, the paper draws implications for deliberation, planning, and control. These practices remain non‑illusory not because the world is indeterminate, but because embedded agents cannot pre‑fix their own futures from within without self‑interference. Openness, on this account, is a constraint of standpoint within deterministic systems, compatible with the IO regime chain and with downstream agency analyses.

• Key move: That perspective-induced openness is a structural condition arising from embedded self-reference - distinct from value degeneracy (Paper 5), epistemic uncertainty, and metaphysica…
• Corpus role: Completes the structural architecture for agency that Paper 5 begins.
• Scope note: Paper 5 explains how underdetermined states resolve.

items.title: Differentiation and Quantum Theory (IO-Compatible Note)
items.summary: Defends structural differentiation against quantum indeterminacy objections.
items.details:
Summary

This short IO‑compatible note addresses a common intuition that quantum mechanics “dissolves” structure by replacing definite classical states with superposition and probabilistic outcomes. It argues that quantum theory relocates structure rather than abolishing it: the wave function is presented as an unusually rigid mathematical object (defined evolution, normalization, interference structure) rather than as an absence of differentiation.

The note reviews two canonical interpretive frames—collapse and Many‑Worlds—to emphasize that neither licenses a shapeless ontology. If collapse occurs, superposition narrows to a single outcome, which presupposes distinguishable possibilities and probabilistic constraint. If Many‑Worlds is correct, branching multiplies outcomes, but branching itself is highly structured (decoherence boundaries, lawful evolution, relational amplitudes).

From there, it runs a thought experiment: a “world” with no distinctions at all. The note argues that even perfect uniformity still carries relational structure (e.g., extension implies spatial relation; persistence implies ordering). Removing all distinction collapses the content of “world” into something indistinguishable from nothing, because identity, state, and fact‑of‑the‑matter require contrast.

Returning to quantum mechanics, the conclusion is that quantum theory exemplifies the persistence of structured differentiation in unfamiliar form. Reality need not be classical, but it must be structured in the minimal sense required for differentiation; quantum formalism, rather than threatening that condition, illustrates how deeply structure persists.

• Key move: Quantum theory, across all its interpretations, never licenses a structureless ontology.
• Corpus role: A defensive stabilization paper for Group I.
• Scope note: The IO framework builds from Difference (Δ) as its foundational regime.

items.title: Lineage Before Selection
items.summary: Establishes structural persistence prior to Darwinian selection.
items.function: Clarifies that lineage continuity precedes evolutionary optimization.
items.details:
Summary

This paper argues that Darwinian selection is not an explanatory primitive. Selection can only operate once there are re-identifiable units that persist across “generation” events. The core claim is therefore upstream: selection presupposes lineage, and lineage presupposes a specific kind of persistence across bifurcation.

The paper starts by unpacking why “selection-first” stories feel plausible: selection is deletion-driven, it can look like order arising from noise, and it appears to require only variation plus differential survival. It then makes explicit what selection actually requires, including a frequently hidden condition: stable units of comparison across bifurcation. Without that re-identifiability, “variants” and “fitness differences” cannot be tracked, accumulated, or inherited.

The central structural move is to define lineage-capable systems as systems that preserve organizational identity across bifurcation via reconstructive inheritance. Boundary formation can localize organization and enable persistence, but boundaries alone do not yield descent. The critical test is what survives bifurcation: whether successor systems can internally reconstitute the organizational constraints that make them re-identifiable as instances of the same organizational kind.

The core argument shows why resemblance without ancestry is insufficient. Selection needs a successor relation that survives repeated bifurcation events; that successor relation depends on internal reconstruction rather than external re-assembly. The paper formalizes this dependency and summarizes how the required inheritance capacity follows from the re-identifiability condition.

Diagnostic cases are used to mark thresholds. Crystal growth illustrates differential persistence without lineage; it propagates constraints but fails reconstructive inheritance. Autocatalytic/RNA-like systems are treated as closer to the lineage threshold, while viruses serve as a confirmation case for parasitic lineage. A final section analyzes breakdown under repeated bifurcation and clarifies what selection explains once lineage exists: distribution of variants, adaptive stabilization, and population-level constraint filtering—without importing teleology or progress.

• Key move: Darwinian selection presupposes lineage-capable systems - systems able to preserve organizational identity across bifurcation via reconstructive inheritance.
• Corpus role: Establishes lineage as structurally prior to selection, creating the analytical gap that Paper 9 fills.
• Scope note: The standard framing of evolution as explanatorily primitive leads to circular origin stories that the IO framework is designed to avoid.

items.title: Abiogenesis: Origin of Lineage
items.summary: Analyzes the emergence of self-maintaining constraint systems.
items.function: Grounds biological lineage within the regime ladder.
items.details:
Summary

This paper reframes abiogenesis as a structural transition: not “the origin of life” in the usual checklist sense, but the emergence of lineage-capable systems. The explanatory target is the appearance of systems that can preserve organizational identity across bifurcation events through reconstructive inheritance.

It begins by arguing that standard “origin of life” framings mis-specify the problem by focusing on metabolism, replication, or genetics. Those can become relevant later, but they do not answer the prior question: how any system can generate successor systems that remain re-identifiable as continuations of the same organizational pattern.

The analysis separates boundary formation from lineage. Boundaries can stabilize persistence by localizing organization and isolating internal dynamics from the environment. But bifurcation—division, fragmentation, fission, templating, or any branching of continuations—creates a harder requirement: after bifurcation, each successor must be able to reconstitute what made the predecessor re-identifiable.

A key distinction is drawn between external and internal reconstruction. In external reconstruction, successor organization is reassembled by the environment or by contingent scaffolding; this can yield persistence but not lineage in the robust sense. In internal reconstruction, constraint architectures within the system generate the processes needed to rebuild organizational specification after bifurcation. Only the latter supports reconstructive inheritance.

The paper defines reconstructive inheritance and uses it to define lineage-capability as a present-tense structural property, not a historical label. It then surveys edge cases (constraint propagation without full lineage) and analyzes breakdown under repeated bifurcation, showing why distributed reconstruction is fragile and why some form of localized constraint carrying becomes necessary downstream.

The result is a cleaned target for origins work: explain how reconstructive inheritance becomes possible under real substrates, rather than treating replication or “genetic code” as the starting point. The paper is explicitly non-biochemical and is intended to provide structural criteria that empirical scenarios must satisfy.

• Key move: Abiogenesis is the emergence of reconstructive inheritance, not the emergence of life.
• Corpus role: Grounds the biological sequence by identifying the origin of lineage and the structural thresholds involved.
• Scope note: Paper 8 establishes that lineage precedes selection.

items.title: Evolution and the Ontological Ladder
items.summary: Explains differential persistence across structural levels.
items.function: Connects evolutionary theory to regime architecture.
items.details:
Summary

This paper provides a structural account of evolution as the temporal population mechanism of the IO regime ladder (Δ → R → I → A → V → M → P). Evolution is treated neither as an optimization story nor as a teleological progression, but as differential persistence under constraint filtering: a deletion-driven process that removes organizational configurations that cannot survive perturbation, especially lineage bifurcation.

The analysis begins with scope discipline: it does not replace biological evolutionary theory or specify genetic mechanisms. Instead, it asks how higher-regime organizational forms become populated at all, given that IO describes regime structure but not the historical process by which those structures come to exist in populations.

Evolution is defined as filtering over populations of organizational configurations, where “inheritance” is the persistence of organizational constraints across lineage bifurcation events. What persists is not semantic content or encoded instruction, but constraint organization sufficient to reconstruct the system’s viable continuation. This links directly to the carrier-first account: fidelity can be a structural property before any representational or code-like interpretation.

A central section explains the emergence of value (I → V) as persistence filtering turned inward. Systems that merely register information without biasing their own transitions toward continuation fail under long-horizon perturbation. Value arises when differential persistence pressure is internalized as differential transition bias: some transitions are treated as stabilizing, others as destabilizing. The paper emphasizes that this is not normativity; it is structural asymmetry in continuation.

Directionality in the ladder is then explained without teleology. Higher regimes are not goals; they are rarer configurations that, once present, can be more stable under extended perturbation. Direction emerges from asymmetric erasure: lower-regime configurations are easier to delete, so populations drift toward the subset that survives longer. The paper also discusses convergent evolution as attraction toward structurally stable configurations under shared constraints.

Overall, the paper positions evolution as the mechanism by which IO’s regime possibilities become historically realized, while maintaining the corpus-wide prohibition on progress narratives, optimization metaphysics, and purpose-as-aim explanations.

• Key move: Evolution is the temporal population mechanism of the IO regime ladder.
• Corpus role: Bridges the biological foundation (Papers 8-9) with the persistence geometry papers (Group III) and the agency papers (Group IV).
• Scope note: The ontology defines what organizational forms are possible; this paper explains why anything ever occupies the higher rungs.

items.title: Constraint Carriers Before Codes
items.summary: Identifies pre-symbolic structural carriers of constraint.
items.function: Grounds biological organization prior to genetic coding.
items.details:
Summary

This paper develops a strictly structural account of proto-genetic inheritance and rejects the default informational metaphor set (“genes as code,” “DNA as message,” “replication as copying”). The central claim is that inheritance precedes replication, and fidelity precedes semantics: systems can transmit organizational constraints across bifurcation without representing, encoding, or interpreting anything.

It begins by defining the minimal structural vocabulary: systems and re-identifiability; organizational constraint sets (K) that delimit viable continuation; background conditions (B); lineage bifurcation events; and structural inheritance as constraint persistence across bifurcation. The goal is to isolate what inheritance must accomplish before any chemical or semantic story is introduced.

The paper then analyzes distributed constraint inheritance regimes, where system-defining constraints are spread across the whole organization. Under bifurcation, successors inherit only fragments of the original constraint organization, causing reconstruction failure. This identifies the core failure mode: inheritance fails not because “information is missing,” but because the organizational constraints required for re-identifiability cannot be reconstituted after branching.

From this failure mode, the paper derives the need for localized constraint carriers: relatively compact, robust structures that concentrate the constraints required to rebuild successor organization. Carriers support reconstruction across bifurcation and enable fidelity without requiring a copying mechanism in the semantic sense. Replication, on this view, is an emergent stabilization of carrier-mediated reconstruction, not the primitive from which inheritance is defined.

A dedicated section clarifies why this account is not semantics-by-stealth. Carrier function is causal and structural: a carrier can constrain reconstruction without standing for anything. Code-like interpretations become available only later, once stabilized carrier systems support systematic reconstitution and can be treated as “instructions” by downstream descriptive frameworks.

The paper ends by stating implications and limits: it does not propose an origin-of-life pathway, but it specifies the structural transition abiogenesis scenarios must achieve, and it links directly to lineage-first and evolution-as-filtering analyses downstream.

• Key move: Inheritance precedes replication, and fidelity precedes semantics.
• Corpus role: Completes the biological foundation group (Papers 8-11) by explaining how constraint persistence gets localized and stabilized across lineage bifurcation.
• Scope note: The corpus rejects representational accounts of meaning throughout (Paper 23) and grounding explanations in symbolic codes would undermine that structural discipline fro…

items.title: Accumulation Without Teleology
items.summary: Defines sedimentation as retained constraint accumulation across lineage without invoking purpose.
items.function: Canonical model of irreversible structural buildup.
items.details:
Summary

“Accumulation Without Teleology” formalizes **constraint sedimentation**: how retained constraint modifications close over one another across lineage, reshaping a system’s identity‑preserving reachability without invoking teleology, optimization, progress, or adaptation. The paper is explicitly downstream of lineage, differential persistence, vulnerability, and degeneracy, and it introduces no new primitives; it tightens the mechanism that later papers use when they talk about history “becoming structure.”

The core move is geometric. The paper treats a continuing system as having an **identity‑preserving reachability set**: the transitions it can still take while remaining re‑identifiable. Within that geometry, “restoration” means reaching earlier viable regions under identity continuity, not merely returning to a convenient external state.

Sedimentation is defined as **recursive constraint closure** within the identity‑preserving network: realized modifications are retained, and later retentions entangle with earlier ones, altering the topology of what remains reachable. This retention can increase local specialization or stability while simultaneously restricting global degrees of freedom.

A key distinction is drawn between sedimentation and irreversibility. Sedimentation is the accumulating mechanism; **irreversibility is a threshold outcome** that occurs when restoration paths are eliminated. Importantly, the paper allows that local flexibility may remain (new or alternative micro‑moves can still exist) while global restoration reachability contracts monotonically across lineage.

The result is a domain‑general operator that later work can apply to biological, institutional, and technical systems: once constraints are retained under identity preservation, the set of identity‑preserving futures is not neutral with respect to history. The paper closes with explicit exclusions and a formal summary designed to prevent drift into normative or evolutionary‑progress readings.

• Key move: Retained constraint modifications recursively close over one another, reshaping reachable state topology without teleology.
• Corpus role: The foundational mechanism paper for Group III.
• Scope note: The corpus analyzes how systems persist, fail, and change - but needed a precise formal account of how history accumulates structurally.

items.title: Invariant Drift and Sedimentation
items.summary: Analyzes gradual modification of identity-maintaining constraints.
items.function: Clarifies how identity evolves without reset.
items.details:
Summary

“Invariant Drift and Sedimentation” asks what happens when the **invariants that sustain re‑identifiability** do not rupture, but drift. Earlier sedimentation analysis often presumes stable identity‑maintaining boundary invariants; this paper treats gradual invariant change as a distinct structural regime that can preserve continuity while altering what “the same system” means over time.

It defines **invariant drift** as gradual modification of identity‑maintaining constraints without a discrete collapse event, and separates it from nearby operations: drift is not reset, not dissolution, and not teleological adaptation. The point is diagnostic clarity: different kinds of boundary change have different consequences for reachability, restoration, and vulnerability.

With the stable‑invariant baseline in view, the paper then tracks how sedimentation behaves under drift. Because sedimentation is indexed to identity‑preserving geometry, drifting invariants can reclassify which retained constraints count as identity‑maintaining versus merely contingent, and can alter which restoration targets remain well‑posed.

The paper develops threshold conditions for identity failure under drift: continuity can persist through gradual change up to a point, but drift can also push the system into regions where re‑identification is no longer warranted, or where restoration becomes structurally ill‑defined because the boundary target is moving. This reframes vulnerability as sensitive not only to accumulated closure, but also to the evolution of the invariants that anchor identity.

Overall, the paper supplies the “boundary evolution” machinery needed to talk precisely about persistence under gradual re‑specification—without smuggling in progress narratives or adaptation‑toward. It also sets up later distinctions between drift, de‑sedimentation (dissolution), and reset as different ways continuity can be lost or reconfigured.

• Key move: Gradual boundary evolution does not automatically erase accumulated constraint, but it alters the frame within which sedimentation is analyzed.
• Corpus role: A technical extension of Paper 12, enabling application of sedimentation analysis to systems whose identity conditions evolve.
• Scope note: The original sedimentation framework assumed invariant stability for formal tractability.

items.title: Constraint Dissolution
items.summary: Formal inverse of sedimentation; examines unwinding and restoration limits.
items.function: Completes the geometry of persistence and recovery.
items.details:
Summary

“Constraint Dissolution” formalizes the inverse‑target of sedimentation: **de‑sedimentation** understood as structural unwinding of retained constraint contributions while maintaining identity continuity. It is downstream of Informational Ontology’s sedimentation, identity, reachability, vulnerability, and degeneracy machinery, and it is written as a completion paper that does not re‑derive those primitives.

The paper first specifies the object of dissolution: not “the system” in general, but the **retained constraint contributions** that have recursively closed over one another across lineage. Dissolution is then defined as an operation that re‑opens reachability—restoring degrees of freedom or restoration paths—by removing, decoupling, or re‑factoring those retained contributions.

A major function of the paper is discrimination. Dissolution is explicitly **not** reset (identity restart), not drift (gradual invariant modification), not restoration (return to a prior state), and not boundary dissolution (loss of re‑identifiability). These distinctions matter because the same surface outcome (“things changed”) can arise from structurally different operations with different vulnerability consequences.

The center of the analysis is feasibility geometry: dissolution is a path problem constrained by dependency depth, entanglement density, redundancy, separability, interfaces/cut‑sets, and slack/tolerance. The paper distinguishes shallow versus deep sediment, isolated versus networked constraints, partial dissolution, and characteristic failure modes (including cascade regimes and dissolution‑induced vulnerability shifts).

Finally, the paper relates dissolution to drift and to degeneracy: drift can sometimes open dissolution feasibility (or deepen sediment), and degeneracy can amplify instability during unwinding. The result is a vocabulary for talking about “unwinding history” in a rigorously non‑teleological way, suitable for later governance/repair discussions without collapsing dissolution into reset or moralized ‘reform’ language.

• Key move: Not all accumulated structure is permanent, but unwinding has structural limits governed by dependency depth and invariant stability.
• Corpus role: Completes the sedimentation trilogy (Papers 12–14) by addressing its structural inverse.
• Scope note: The governance and alignment arcs repeatedly confront situations where systems need to change deeply - governance regimes need to be reformed, institutions restructured,…

items.title: Irreversibility Thresholds in Replicating Systems
items.summary: Identifies topology-based thresholds where recall collapses under replication.
items.details:
Summary

“Irreversibility Thresholds in Replicating Systems” develops a structural account of why replication and recall are asymmetric in distributed digital ecologies (e.g., malware propagation, peer‑to‑peer mirroring, leak diffusion, and version‑control graphs). Replication succeeds under an **existential condition** (at least one viable locus persists and can reseed); recall—understood as containment reconstitution—requires a **universal condition** (all loci and all reconstitution pathways inconsistent with the prior boundary state must be eliminated or neutralized).

Using IO’s boundary and reachability framing, the paper defines replication, recall, containment, and boundary state operationally, then identifies a practical irreversibility threshold: the point where restoration of the pre‑diffusion boundary state is no longer realistically re‑instantiable under bounded enumeration, bounded coordination, bounded access, and bounded verification capacity.

A key clarification is the distinction between **reset and repair**. Many operational reports treat “reset” actions (credential rotation, re‑imaging, revocation) as if they were repairs that restore the original boundary. The paper argues that after a diffusion threshold, resets often function as construction of a new boundary state sustained by ongoing enforcement, not recovery of the old one.

The analysis emphasizes the **verification burden** on recall. Even if most loci are removed, containment reconstitution binds to the hardest‑to‑enumerate edge cases: unseen mirrors, offline copies, delayed re‑emergence pathways, and verification gaps. As a result, the system can cross into a regime where containment is no longer a finite recovery task but an indefinite maintenance task.

The paper closes with case‑ecology dynamics and falsifiers/disconfirming conditions, keeping the claim structural rather than alarmist: the threshold is not “everything is irreversible,” but a specific condition under which restoration becomes structurally unattainable and must be replaced by ongoing boundary stabilization.

• Key move: There is a structural threshold in replicating systems at which B0 restoration becomes practically infeasible under bounded enumeration, coordination, access, and verification cap…
• Corpus role: An applied instantiation of the irreversibility framework from Papers 12–14, bringing the abstract constraint topology into contact with a concrete and practically signi…
• Scope note: The replication–recall asymmetry is widely observed in cybersecurity, information governance, and AI model distribution, but has lacked a precise structural characteriza…

items.title: Acceleration and Degeneracy
items.summary: Demonstrates identity-bound reachability collapse under differential scaling.
items.function: Formal collapse model under acceleration; replaces Execution–Intervention.
items.details:
Summary

“Acceleration and Degeneracy” analyzes how acceleration changes the persistence conditions of **degenerate systems**—systems with multiple identity‑preserving constraint sequences available in their topology. Degeneracy is not treated as mere redundancy; it is structural multiplicity of restoration‑compatible pathways.

The central claim is that acceleration does not automatically destroy degeneracy. Instead, irreversibility can arise when scaling under acceleration is **asymmetric**: perturbation propagation scales faster than restoration propagation. In that case, identity‑preserving alternatives may exist in principle but become **unreachable within identity continuity** at the moment boundary crossing occurs.

This produces a specific mechanism: **reachability collapse under differential scaling**. The system crosses its identity boundary before degenerate restoration pathways can activate, so failure is not explained by “no alternatives,” but by temporal/scale constraints that eliminate feasible paths.

The paper distinguishes this account from standard stability/control framings by focusing on topology‑preserving restoration and identity‑bound reachability, not merely trajectory response. It then supplies worked illustrations (e.g., high‑frequency market collapse dynamics, epidemic spread versus health‑system restoration rates, and automated deployment/rollback failure) to show the same scaling logic across domains.

Finally, the paper formalizes definitions and propositions, clarifies scope limitations, and sketches operationalization/measurement variables so the mechanism can be diagnosed without importing normative evaluation. The outcome is a reusable lens for when “speed” turns existing alternatives into non‑options.

• Key move: Acceleration alone does not produce collapse.
• Corpus role: Closes Group III by integrating the sedimentation framework with the question of operational tempo.
• Scope note: Many sociotechnical systems - financial markets, public health systems, distributed software infrastructure - now operate under accelerated propagation regimes where per…

items.title: The Silence Limit
items.summary: Argues purposive constraint cannot persist absent living originators.
items.function: Teleological boundary condition of automation regimes.
items.details:
Summary

“The Silence Limit” establishes a structural limit on purposive organization within IO: **purpose cannot arise or persist** in purely informational systems absent participation in a living constraint ecology capable of originating non‑surrogable stakes, loss, and irreversibility. The claim is ontological (about regime conditions), not empirical forecasting, and the paper explicitly avoids alignment prescriptions, risk claims, and moral status arguments.

Working downstream of the regime ladder, the paper introduces a **silence condition**: when constraint originators are absent, purposive structure collapses without remainder even if informational organization persists. In IO terms, the system may still exhibit information and even complex organization, but the M→P regime transition fails to stabilize because the relevant value/meaning constraints are no longer grounded in origination of stakes.

The paper argues that self‑reconfiguration is insufficient to rescue purpose in this setting. Without living constraint originators, purposive constraints become surrogable and can be re‑weighted, copied, or optimized away without generating the non‑substitutable loss structure required for stable purposive continuity.

A proof‑sketch style section formalizes why purposive persistence binds to constraint origination rather than to informational complexity alone, and it clarifies what counts as “living constraint ecology” in the strict structural sense used by the corpus (not a claim about consciousness or intention).

The paper ends with non‑normative implications: it is a boundary condition for what kinds of systems can sustain purpose under the IO definitions, and it functions as a guardrail against silently importing teleology or ‘goal persistence’ assumptions into downstream AI/governance discussions.

• Key move: Purpose cannot arise or persist in purely informational systems absent participation in a living constraint ecology capable of originating non-surrogable stakes, loss, and irrever…
• Corpus role: The teleological boundary condition for the entire governance and AI alignment arc.
• Scope note: The corpus builds to this point methodically through Groups I–IV.

items.title: Agency, Salience, and Free Will
items.summary: Structural account of underdetermination and valuation resolution.
items.details:
• Key move: Agency is real, structurally definable, and doesn't require psychology or metaphysical indeterminism.
• Corpus role: Central to Group IV.
• Scope note: Agency and free will are among the most contested concepts in philosophy, precisely because they are usually defined either through psychology (felt experience of choosi…

items.title: Salience, Manipulation, and Control — A Structural Account
items.summary: Analyzes manipulation as salience restructuring rather than agency bypass.
items.details:
• Key move: Manipulation is salience restructuring, not deception.
• Corpus role: Released (DOI available).
• Scope note: As AI systems become the primary mediators of information environments, the salience-restructuring mode of influence becomes dominant.

items.title: Saturation and Sovereignty: Addiction as a Structural Constraint Regime
items.pdf: /papers/15. Saturation And Sovereignty - Addiction As A Structural Constraint Regime-FINAL.pdf
items.summary: Models addiction as trajectory-level salience monopoly.
items.details:
• Key move: Addiction is constraint saturation realized through salience monopoly.
• Corpus role: Released (DOI available).
• Scope note: Addiction is one of the most contested and practically consequential domains where standard explanations systematically fail.

items.title: Salience Collapse and Meaning Saturation
items.summary: Diagnoses systemic degradation of meaningful differentiation under overload.
items.details:
• Key move: Salience collapse is a distinct structural failure mode - recognizable ex ante through the collapse of backgrounding, staging, and deferral mechanisms - that produces reactive, br…
• Corpus role: Generalizes Paper 20's saturation mechanism to field-wide and collective contexts.
• Scope note: The corpus needed a macro-level account to pair with the individual-level account in Paper 20.

items.title: Salience Misalignment
items.summary: Explains persistent misalignment without malfunction.
items.details:
• Key move: Salience misalignment is not malfunction.
• Corpus role: A diagnostic framework connecting the salience mechanics (Papers 18-21) to the governance arc.
• Scope note: Many governance failure analyses focus on error, corruption, or misaligned incentives.

items.title: Meaning Without Semantics
items.summary: Treats meaning as structural constraint effect rather than representation.
items.details:
• Key move: Meaning does not begin with symbols, reference, truth, or norms.
• Corpus role: Completes the V → M transition in the regime ladder (Paper 1).
• Scope note: The corpus rejects representational accounts at every level.

items.title: Purpose Without Teleology
items.summary: Explains purpose as emergent trajectory constraint without final causes.
items.details:
• Key move: Once meaning exists in a system that persists under constraint and ordering, purpose is structurally structurally stabilized.
• Corpus role: Completes the M → P transition and thereby completes the regime ladder derivation begun in Paper 1.
• Scope note: Without a non-teleological account of purpose, the corpus cannot explain purposive behavior in organisms, institutions, and agents without importing assumptions it's des…

items.title: Vulnerability as a Structural Condition: Consequence Without Normativity
items.summary: Defines vulnerability as lineage-relevant susceptibility to irreversible loss.
items.details:
• Key move: Consequence is structurally prior to ethics.
• Corpus role: The foundational definitions paper for the entire governance arc.
• Scope note: Papers on responsibility, witnessing, legitimacy, and governance all presuppose that consequences land somewhere - that loss is real and matters to a system's future.

items.title: Witnessing Under Vulnerability: Consequence Termination Under Scale and Tempo
items.summary: Formalizes witnessing as termination of consequence within vulnerable loci.
items.details:
• Key move: The consolidation paper for the witnessing concept across the IO corpus.
• Corpus role: The pivotal paper for the applied governance arc.
• Scope note: Witnessing was implicit across many prior papers but never formally isolated.

items.title: Witness Conditions
items.summary: Specifies structural requirements for witnessing under scale and AI mediation.
items.details:
• Key move: Responsibility is structurally scaled, not metaphysically absolute.
• Corpus role: Connects the agency mechanics of Group IV to the governance architecture of Group V.
• Scope note: The corpus needed a bridge between its structural account of agency (Group IV) and its analysis of governance regimes (Group V).

items.title: Partial Agency and Mixed Responsibility
items.summary: Analyzes distributed constraint contribution under scale.
items.details:
• Key move: Ethical regimes are coordination regimes, not normative authorities.
• Corpus role: Bridges the purpose and agency papers (Group IV) with the institutional analysis of governance.
• Scope note: Without a structural account of ethics, the corpus would need either to treat ethics as normatively primitive (which would violate the non-normative discipline throughou…

items.title: Ethics as Emergent Constraint Regimes
items.summary: Explains ethics as stabilized structural regimes.
items.details:
• Key move: Markets are neither ethical nor unethical.
• Corpus role: An application paper demonstrating the framework's reach into economic analysis.
• Scope note: Markets appear throughout governance analysis as the default non-state coordination regime.

items.title: Responsibility, Law, and Moral Practice
items.summary: Bridges structural responsibility and institutional practice.
items.details:
• Key move: Legitimacy is finite, exhaustible, and consumed through use rather than error.
• Corpus role: The connective paper linking the witnessing framework (Paper 26) to the applied governance papers on law-to-logistics (Paper 31), the handover protocol (Paper 32), and t…
• Scope note: Legitimacy is a key concept throughout governance theory but is typically treated as a matter of belief, consent, or normative justification.

items.title: From Law to Logistics: How AI Thins Coercive Regimes
items.summary: Diagnoses AI-mediated thinning of coercive regimes.
items.details:
• Key move: The paper is explicitly a structural map rather than a completed argument - an orienting reference frame that stabilizes key distinctions (commitment, discretion, attribution, log…
• Corpus role: The architectural reference document for the governance and alignment sub-arc.
• Scope note: The governance arc needed a stable conceptual map that could orient the cascade of more specific papers.

items.title: The Handover Protocol: Formalizing Necessary Regime Thinning Under Automation
items.summary: Distinguishes legitimate automation from degenerative thinning.
items.details:
• Key move: AI does not abolish coercive regimes - it thins them.
• Corpus role: The first major applied governance paper, grounding the abstract witnessing and legitimacy framework in a concrete and consequential domain.
• Scope note: Coercive AI systems - risk-scoring for bail, sentencing, parole; automated screening and flagging; algorithmic enforcement - are among the most practically significant g…

items.title: Legitimacy as a Finite Structural Resource
items.summary: Analyzes structural depletion of legitimacy.
items.details:
• Key move: Automation itself does not constitute regime failure.
• Corpus role: Released (DOI available).
• Scope note: The corpus diagnoses regime thinning extensively; this paper asks what legitimate thinning looks like.

items.title: Authority After Commitment
items.summary: Maps authority topology after constraint commitment.
items.details:
• Key move: structurally constrained handover is not sufficient for accountability preservation across regime boundaries.
• Corpus role: Operates immediately downstream of Paper 33 (The Handover Protocol).
• Scope note: The governance arc needed to go beyond diagnosing thinning and to analyze what happens after legitimate thinning - the structural residues and second-order failures that…

items.title: Authority, Responsibility, and Force
items.summary: Explains escalation when witnessing collapses.
items.details:
• Key move: The diagnostic test is witnessing: when specific constraint-events are contested, can the regime produce a terminal locus that identifies the commitment-event that authorized the…
• Corpus role: Completes the governance arc that runs from Paper 26 (Witnessing) through Papers 31–34.
• Scope note: This paper is the governance arc's culminating diagnostic - the account that explains why every mechanism designed to preserve accountability (audits, transparency, huma…

items.title: Markets as Multi-Agent Constraint Stabilizers
items.summary: Treats markets as distributed constraint coordination systems.
items.details:
• Key move: Responsibility, liability, and risk ownership can persist after authority-bearing justification has disappeared, but they cannot preserve alignment in the authority-bearing sense.
• Corpus role: Completes the governance conceptual framework.
• Scope note: The governance arc needed to close the conceptual gap between structural authority and legal/institutional accountability mechanisms.

items.title: Informational Ontology and AI Alignment
items.summary: Applies ontology to alignment constraints.
items.details:
• Key move: Alignment is a question of structural compatibility among interacting systems, not primarily a problem of value specification, preference matching, or control optimization.
• Corpus role: The entry point into the AI alignment arc.
• Scope note: Serves as a bridge paper, applying the IO framework's agency and salience mechanics to the alignment domain before the full alignment arc develops.

items.title: Conditions of Possibility for Human–AI Alignment
items.summary: Identifies necessary structural coherence conditions.
items.details:
• Key move: Many alignment intuitions are structurally inadmissible prior to implementation because they presuppose conditions that are mutually incompatible once meaning, purpose, and ethics…
• Corpus role: The entry point into Group VI for readers coming from alignment backgrounds.
• Scope note: As the primary survey-level application paper in the AI alignment arc, this paper situates the entire arc relative to existing alignment discourse and establishes why st…

items.title: Consequence Path Audits
items.summary: Diagnostic framework for tracing consequence chains.
items.details:
• Key move: Alignment remains an open modal question.
• Corpus role: Sits between the critical work of Paper 38 and the governance-level analysis of Paper 40.
• Scope note: The alignment arc needed a paper that examined the problem space before proposing diagnostics or governance responses.

items.title: Working as Designed
items.summary: Explains alignment failure without malfunction.
items.details:
• Key move: "Alignment" is a regime-relative concept whose coherence depends on the continued presence - or explicit relocation - of witnessing and answerability.
• Corpus role: The convergence paper for the governance and alignment arcs.
• Scope note: The governance and alignment arcs needed a terminal paper that stated the structural limit clearly.

items.title: Alignment After Witnessing
items.summary: Explores governance after witnessing collapse.
items.details:
• Key move: Epistemic practices within IO arise as structural necessities, not normative aspirations.
• Corpus role: The epistemological completion paper for the entire corpus.
• Scope note: A systematic philosophical framework needs an explicit account of its own epistemic status.

items.title: Structural Compatibility — Diagnostic Essay
items.summary: Diagnoses trajectory compatibility between human and AI systems.
items.details:
• Key move: Logic is not dethroned or weakened - it is correctly located.
• Corpus role: The methodological completion paper, paired with Paper 41 (Epistemic Regimes) to close the framework's self-accounting arc.
• Scope note: An ontological framework that makes structural claims needs to address its relationship to logic explicitly - particularly one that refuses to treat logic as foundationa…

items.title: Can AI Participate in Philosophical Method
items.summary: Analyzes AI’s structural role in philosophical reasoning.
items.function: Defines limits of AI participation in theory construction.
items.details:
• Key move: Analyzes where AI can contribute to philosophical work (search, compression, adversarial testing, formalization support) without being treated as an authority.
• Corpus role: Methodological boundary-setting for AI-assisted theory construction.
• Scope note: Focuses on structural participation conditions and failure modes, not on marketing claims about AI capability.

items.title: Epistemic Regimes
items.summary: Structural account of truth-diagnostic regimes.
items.details:
• Key move: Defines epistemic regimes as stabilized truth-diagnostic practices with identifiable success and failure surfaces.
• Corpus role: Provides the knowledge-stabilization layer that the methodology and governance papers rely on when they diagnose rather than prescribe.
• Scope note: Structural account of how truth-tracking is maintained under stress; not a general theory of justification.

items.title: The Truth Protocol
items.summary: Formal maintenance doctrine for epistemic regimes under stress.
items.details:
• Key move: Specifies maintenance constraints for epistemic regimes (self-vulnerability, auditability, and controlled exposure to error signals).
• Corpus role: Operational doctrine for keeping epistemic machinery honest as scale, speed, and incentives increase.
• Scope note: Focuses on regime upkeep and diagnostic rigor rather than on proving first principles.

items.title: Logic as a Regime, Not a Foundation
items.pdf: /papers/13. Logic as a Regime, Not a Foundation_REVISED-FINAL.pdf
items.summary: Treats logic as stabilized regime rather than metaphysical primitive.
items.details:
• Key move: Treats logic as a regime (a stabilized constraint practice) rather than as a metaphysical foundation.
• Corpus role: Closes the self-accounting arc by locating how logical validity functions inside an IO-style structural framework.
• Scope note: Reframes the status of logic for this corpus; it is not an anti-logic argument.
